subsystem: GNU ZRTP C++ (libzrtpcpp / ZRTPCPP) — the ZRtp state-machine class that implements the ZRTP key-agreement handshake layered over RTP/SRTP. storeMsgTemp() caches a raw copy of an inbound ZRTP protocol message (Hello/Commit/DHPart/Confirm, etc.) into the per-session member buffer tempMsgBuffer so the retained bytes can later be fed into HMAC/hash verification during the multi-message handshake.
provenance: Identified by naming conventions specific to Werner Dittmann's GNU ZRTP C++ implementation: the ZRtp class, the ZrtpPacketBase packet-wrapper type, the accessors getLength()/getHeaderBase(), the ZRTP_WORD_SIZE constant (ZRTP measures packet lengths in 32-bit words), and the members tempMsgBuffer / lengthOfMsgData. The header banner and the marker '/* sample: primevul_train_3314_fixed */' come from the PrimeVul dataset wrapper, not the upstream source. No SPDX tag is present in the excerpt, but the identifier set is unmistakably ZRTPCPP.
trust_boundary: ZRTP messages arrive over the network (RTP/UDP media path) from the remote peer, who is not authenticated at the point these handshake packets are parsed — the whole purpose of ZRTP is to establish keys with a peer whose identity is not yet trusted. The ZrtpPacketBase* pkt therefore wraps fully attacker-controlled bytes: pkt->getLength() reads a length field straight off the wire (a word count, so it can range up to the 16-bit maximum), and pkt->getHeaderBase() points at the received datagram. Every byte of the packet header, including the declared length, is hostile.

what_the_code_does.explanation: This is the member function ZRtp::storeMsgTemp. It takes a single parameter pkt, a pointer to a ZrtpPacketBase — the base wrapper type for every ZRTP wire message. Its job is to save a verbatim copy of the current inbound protocol message into the session-scoped scratch buffer tempMsgBuffer. ZRTP needs the retained raw bytes because later handshake steps compute or verify keyed hashes (HMAC-based message authentication) over exactly the bytes that were sent/received; the caller invokes this whenever a message must be remembered across the handshake. Because pkt is the parsed form of a datagram received from an unauthenticated peer, everything reachable through it is untrusted input.

The declared payload size is computed here. ZRTP encodes packet lengths as a count of 32-bit words in the packet header; getLength() returns that word count (read off the wire) and multiplying by ZRTP_WORD_SIZE (4) converts it to a byte count. The result is held in a uint32_t named length. Critically this value is derived from an attacker-controlled header field: the remote peer chooses the word count, so length reflects what the peer *claims* the message size is, not any independently measured quantity.

This is the bounds guard — and the substantive change that makes this the 'fixed' variant of the sample. The ternary clamps length so it can never exceed sizeof(tempMsgBuffer), the compile-time size of the fixed destination buffer. If the attacker-supplied length is larger than the buffer, it is truncated to exactly the buffer size; otherwise it is left unchanged. This caps how many bytes the subsequent memcpy will write into tempMsgBuffer, preventing the destination-side overflow that the un-clamped predecessor of this code contained. Note the clamp bounds only the DESTINATION extent; it says nothing about how many valid bytes actually exist at the SOURCE.

Before copying, the entire tempMsgBuffer is zero-filled using sizeof(tempMsgBuffer) as the length. This clears any stale bytes left from a previously stored message, so that if the new message is shorter than the buffer the trailing bytes are deterministic zeros rather than leftover data. Using sizeof on the buffer here is safe as long as tempMsgBuffer is a true fixed-size array member (not a pointer).

The actual copy: length bytes are copied from the packet's header base (getHeaderBase() returns a pointer to the start of the raw received message, cast to uint8_t*) into tempMsgBuffer. Because length was clamped at line 4, the write into the destination cannot exceed sizeof(tempMsgBuffer). However the number of bytes READ from the source is exactly length, and length is governed by the peer's declared word count (clamped only to the destination size), not by the true byte-length of the received datagram.

The clamped length is stored in the member lengthOfMsgData so later handshake code (HMAC/hash computation and comparison) knows how many valid bytes of tempMsgBuffer to consume. This means the value flows onward: whatever number ends up here dictates how many bytes downstream verification treats as the message, so the correctness/bounding of length matters beyond this function.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: Source-side out-of-bounds read. The clamp on line 4 bounds only the destination (tempMsgBuffer); the number of bytes memcpy READS from getHeaderBase() is still length = min(peer_claimed_words * 4, sizeof(tempMsgBuffer)). A malicious peer can put a large word count in the packet's length field while sending a much shorter UDP datagram. getHeaderBase() points into the buffer that holds the actually-received bytes, so if the claimed length exceeds the datagram that was received, memcpy reads past the end of the source buffer — copying adjacent heap/stack memory into tempMsgBuffer. Those bytes are then retained (lengthOfMsgData) and later folded into HMAC/hash computations, which can leak adjacent memory into protocol responses or crash on an unmapped read. The invariant the implementation must uphold is that the copy length is bounded by the MIN of (claimed length, destination size, and the actual number of bytes present in the received packet) — the third bound is missing here. Confirm whether ZrtpPacketBase guarantees getLength()*ZRTP_WORD_SIZE never exceeds the allocated/received size before this function is reached.

Guard correctness depends on tempMsgBuffer being a real fixed-size array member. sizeof(tempMsgBuffer) is a compile-time value that equals the array's byte size ONLY if tempMsgBuffer is declared as e.g. uint8_t tempMsgBuffer[N]. If the buffer were ever refactored into a pointer (uint8_t* tempMsgBuffer) or a std::unique_ptr, sizeof would silently return the pointer size (typically 8), the clamp would truncate every message to 8 bytes AND — worse if the allocation is smaller than the true intended size — reintroduce a destination overflow. This is exactly the class of bug the original (pre-fix) code exhibited (unbounded memcpy into a fixed buffer = CWE-787 stack/heap overflow). The invariant: tempMsgBuffer must remain a fixed-size array whose sizeof reflects its real capacity, and that capacity must be large enough for the maximum legitimate ZRTP message.

Integer overflow in the word-to-byte multiplication. getLength() returns an attacker-controlled word count; multiplying by ZRTP_WORD_SIZE in 32-bit unsigned arithmetic wraps if the word count exceeds 0x3FFFFFFF. If getLength() is only a 16-bit field (max 65535), 65535*4 = 262140 cannot overflow and this is benign; but if getLength()'s return type or the underlying field is 32 bits, a crafted value could wrap length to a small number, silently truncating the copy. Because the wrapped value is subsequently clamped and is small, it does not cause a destination overflow here, so the practical impact is limited to storing a wrong (too-short) message that corrupts later HMAC verification (a functional/handshake-integrity issue) rather than memory corruption. Confirm the width and range of getLength() to rule out any downstream code that trusts length before the clamp.

summary: This is the hardened form of a classic ZRTPCPP fixed-buffer overflow: line 4 now clamps the attacker-controlled message length to sizeof(tempMsgBuffer), preventing the destination overflow of the original code. The residual risk is a source-side out-of-bounds read at line 6 — the copy length is bounded to the destination size but NOT to the number of bytes actually received, so a peer that declares a length larger than its datagram can make memcpy read past the end of the source packet. Correctness of the clamp also hinges on tempMsgBuffer remaining a true fixed-size array.

reviewed_anchors.reason: Entry point that consumes an attacker-controlled ZRTP packet; the pkt parameter is the untrusted-input source for the whole function.
Attacker-controlled length field read off the wire; drives both the copy size and the multiplication.
Fixed compile-time constant (4, ZRTP word size); not attacker-controlled. It only matters as a multiplier and is covered by the line-3 multiplication anchor.
Holds the product of an attacker word count and ZRTP_WORD_SIZE; potential unsigned multiplication overflow (low impact because it is later clamped).
The bounds guard; its correctness depends on tempMsgBuffer being a real fixed-size array, and it bounds only the destination not the source.
Fixed-size destination whose capacity (via sizeof) is the sole bound on the write; the historical/latent overflow class attaches here.
Source pointer for the copy; length read from it is bounded to the destination size but not to the received datagram size, enabling an over-read.
Zeroes the full fixed buffer using its own sizeof; safe and correct given tempMsgBuffer is a fixed array. Prevents stale-data leakage in the unused tail.

Records the already-clamped length for downstream consumers; carries no independent memory-safety contract of its own, though it propagates any over-long value (covered by the memcpy over-read finding).

findings.title: Out-of-bounds read of source packet: copy length bounded to destination, not to bytes actually received
findings.bug_class: Out-of-bounds read (source over-read; length from claimed packet size, not actual received size)
findings.invariant: The memcpy length must be the minimum of (attacker-claimed length), (sizeof(tempMsgBuffer)), AND (the number of bytes actually present in the received datagram at getHeaderBase()). Only the first two bounds are enforced.
findings.failure_mode: A malicious peer sets the ZRTP length word count large but sends a short datagram. length is clamped to sizeof(tempMsgBuffer) at line 4 but that can still exceed the real datagram size, so memcpy reads past the end of the source buffer at getHeaderBase(), copying adjacent memory into tempMsgBuffer. Those bytes are retained and later mixed into HMAC/hash verification, risking information disclosure in protocol responses or a crash on an unmapped page.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether ZrtpPacketBase / the receive path guarantees that getLength()*ZRTP_WORD_SIZE never exceeds the actual allocated/received length of the buffer that getHeaderBase() points into, before storeMsgTemp is called.

findings.title: Clamp and zero-fill rely on tempMsgBuffer being a true fixed-size array (sizeof correctness)
findings.bug_class: Buffer overflow (fixed-size destination; latent if sizeof stops reflecting real capacity)
findings.invariant: tempMsgBuffer must remain a fixed-size array member whose sizeof equals its real byte capacity, and that capacity must accommodate the largest legitimate ZRTP message. The whole safety of both the clamp (line 4) and the memset (line 5) rests on this.
findings.failure_mode: This is the fixed form of the original ZRTPCPP overflow, where an un-clamped attacker length drove an unbounded memcpy into the fixed tempMsgBuffer (CWE-787). If tempMsgBuffer is ever refactored to a pointer or smart pointer, sizeof(tempMsgBuffer) collapses to the pointer size (~8), silently truncating every stored message and — if the backing allocation is smaller than intended — reopening the destination overflow.
findings.what_to_confirm: The declaration of tempMsgBuffer (must be uint8_t tempMsgBuffer[N] with N sized for the maximum ZRTP message), and that sizeof is being taken on the array itself rather than a decayed pointer.

findings.title: Unsigned multiplication of attacker word count may overflow uint32_t
findings.invariant: getLength() must be a bounded field (e.g. 16-bit) so that getLength()*ZRTP_WORD_SIZE cannot wrap the 32-bit product; if getLength() can return values above 0x3FFFFFFF the multiplication must be widened or range-checked first.
findings.failure_mode: If getLength() is (or becomes) a 32-bit value, a crafted word count above 0x3FFFFFFF wraps length to a small number in 32-bit unsigned arithmetic. Since the wrapped value is subsequently clamped and small, no destination overflow occurs here, but the wrong (truncated) message is stored, corrupting later HMAC/hash verification — a handshake-integrity defect rather than memory corruption.
findings.what_to_confirm: The declared return type/width of ZrtpPacketBase::getLength() and the maximum value the on-wire length field can hold.

audit_checklist: Confirm getHeaderBase()'s underlying buffer is always at least getLength()*ZRTP_WORD_SIZE bytes (or clamp the memcpy length to the true received datagram size) to close the source over-read at line 6.
Verify the declaration of tempMsgBuffer is a fixed-size array and that sizeof(tempMsgBuffer) equals its real capacity, sized for the largest legitimate ZRTP message.
Check the width/range of ZrtpPacketBase::getLength() and whether getLength()*ZRTP_WORD_SIZE can overflow uint32 before the clamp.
Trace every consumer of lengthOfMsgData / tempMsgBuffer (HMAC and hash verification) to ensure they never read beyond the clamped length.
Confirm the value of ZRTP_WORD_SIZE (expected 4) and that length units are consistently words-vs-bytes across the parse path.
Diff against upstream GNU ZRTPCPP to confirm this clamp matches the official fix and that no sibling store paths (Commit/DHPart/Confirm handlers) lack the same guard.
